audit_status.source_base: Greek Acts text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for voyage to Rome, storm, Paul warning, angelic assurance, shipwreck, Eucharistic meal imagery, all saved/healed alive.

verses.restored: When it was decided that we should sail away to Italy, they handed Paul and some other prisoners to a centurion named Julius of the Augustan cohort.
Embarking in a ship of Adramyttium about to sail to places along Asia, we put to sea, Aristarchus a Macedonian from Thessalonica being with us.
The next day we put in at Sidon. Julius treated Paul kindly and allowed him to go to friends to receive care.
Putting out from there, we sailed under the lee of Cyprus because the winds were contrary.
Sailing through the sea along Cilicia and Pamphylia, we came to Myra of Lycia.
There the centurion found an Alexandrian ship sailing to Italy and put us aboard it.
Sailing slowly for many days and coming with difficulty off Cnidus, the wind not permitting us, we sailed under the lee of Crete off Salmone.
Coasting along it with difficulty, we came to a certain place called Fair Havens, near which was the city Lasea.
Since considerable time had passed and the voyage was already dangerous, because even the Fast had already gone by, Paul advised them,
saying to them, Men, I behold that the voyage is about to be with hardship and much loss, not only of cargo and ship, but also of our lives/souls.
But the centurion was persuaded more by the pilot and shipowner than by the things spoken by Paul.
Because the harbor was unsuitable for wintering, the majority advised putting out from there, if somehow they might reach Phoenix, a harbor of Crete facing southwest and northwest, and winter there.
When a south wind blew gently, supposing they had obtained their purpose, they lifted anchor and sailed along Crete close inshore.
Not long after, a violent wind called the Northeaster rushed down from it.
The ship was caught and not able to face the wind, so giving way, we were driven.
Running under the lee of a small island called Cauda, we were barely able to secure the skiff.
After hoisting it, they used helps to undergird the ship. Fearing they might run aground on the Syrtis, lowering the gear, they were driven.
Being violently storm-tossed, the next day they began throwing cargo overboard.
On the third day they threw the ship's tackle overboard with their own hands.
When neither sun nor stars appeared for many days, and no small storm lay upon us, from then on all hope that we would be saved/healed was being taken away.
After much abstinence from food, Paul stood in their midst and said, Men, you should indeed have obeyed me and not put out from Crete, and so avoided this hardship and loss.
And now I urge you to take courage, for there will be no loss of life/soul among you, but only of the ship.
For this night a messenger of the Divine to whom I belong and whom I serve stood by me,
saying, Do not fear, Paul; it is necessary for you to stand before Caesar. And look, the Divine has graciously given you all those sailing with you.
Therefore take courage, men, for I trust the Divine that it will be thus, according to the way it has been spoken to me.
But it is necessary for us to run aground on some island.
When the fourteenth night came, as we were being driven about in the Adriatic, around midnight the sailors suspected that some land was approaching them.
Taking soundings, they found twenty fathoms; going a little farther, again taking soundings, they found fifteen fathoms.
Fearing we might run somewhere onto rocky places, they threw four anchors from the stern and were praying for day to come.
As the sailors were seeking to flee from the ship and had lowered the skiff into the sea under pretense of laying out anchors from the bow,
Paul said to the centurion and soldiers, Unless these remain in the ship, you are not able to be saved/healed.
Then the soldiers cut away the ropes of the skiff and let it fall off.
Until day was about to come, Paul was urging all to take food, saying, Today is the fourteenth day you have continued waiting without food, taking nothing.
Therefore I urge you to take food, for this is for your saving/healing; for not a hair from the head of any of you will perish.
Saying these things and taking bread, he gave thanks to the Divine before all, and breaking it, began to eat.
All became encouraged, and they themselves took food.
We were in all two hundred seventy-six souls/lives in the ship.
Having been satisfied with food, they lightened the ship, throwing the wheat into the sea.
When day came, they did not recognize the land, but noticed a certain bay with a beach, onto which they planned, if able, to drive the ship.
Casting off the anchors, they left them in the sea, at the same time loosening the bands of the rudders, and raising the foresail to the wind, they held toward the beach.
But striking a place between two seas, they ran the vessel aground. The bow stuck and remained immovable, but the stern was being broken by the force of the waves.
The soldiers' plan was to kill the prisoners, lest any, swimming away, should escape.
But the centurion, wishing to save/heal Paul, prevented them from their intention and commanded those able to swim to throw themselves off first and get to land,
and the rest, some on planks and some on things from the ship. And thus it happened that all were brought safely through onto the land.
